context: Psalm 30 is David's song of thanksgiving after deliverance. He remembers crying to God from the depths and being lifted up, and he draws a conclusion from his own story: God's anger lasts a moment, his favour a lifetime, and grief gives way to morning joy.
reflection: If you are in a night of trial, think of the morning. Your head may be crowned with thorny troubles now, and your hands may be full of cares, but wait a little longer. How small our troubles will seem when we look back on them. Seen from the middle, they look immense; seen from the far side, they are light and momentary. However dark the night, the morning comes. It may be all trial now, but it will not always be so. What does it matter that weeping endures for a night, when joy comes in the morning? Go on steadily, and live on that sure hope.
today: When today's trouble looks immense, name it as a night, not a climate. Nights end.

context: In the Sermon on the Mount, Jesus teaches his disciples not to be anxious about food and clothing. He points to the birds, who neither sow nor store, yet are fed by God — and he calls God their heavenly Father, and yours.
reflection: Father — what a precious word that is. Here is authority veiled so sweetly in love that the crown is forgotten in the face of the King who wears it. Here is affection that does what friendship cannot do and mere kindness will not attempt; a father's heart and hand must act for his children. If an earthly father watches over his children with unceasing care, how much more your heavenly Father. The one who can say 'Abba, Father' has uttered better music than angels reach. There is all I can ask in that one word, all my needs can demand, all my wishes can desire, when I can say 'Father.'
today: Once today, begin a prayer with the single word 'Father' and let it stand a moment before you ask for anything.

context: The opening chapter of Genesis describes God creating and ordering the world. On the first day he separates light from darkness, names them Day and Night, and the first evening and morning pass — a rhythm woven into creation from the beginning.
reflection: If light and darkness divided even the first day, it is little wonder my circumstances change from sunshine to midnight. It will not always be noon, even in the soul. What our heavenly Father ordains is wise and good, so learn to be content with this divine order, willing to receive both seasons from his hand. There is beauty in sunrise and in sunset; sing of both, and glorify the Lord. Believe that the night is as useful as the day: the dews of grace fall heavily in the night of sorrow, and the stars of promise shine amid the darkness of grief. Every hour has its work — labour by day, watch by night.
today: Whatever season this day is, bright or dim, treat it as rhythm rather than accident. Do the work that belongs to this hour.

context: Hebrews closes with practical instruction for believers under pressure. The writer tells them to keep their lives free from the love of money and to be content, resting the whole command on one promise from God: I will never leave you nor forsake you.
reflection: 'He has said' — if we can grasp those words by faith, we hold an all-conquering weapon. What fear will not fall before this arrow from the bow of God's promise? The distresses of life and the trials on every side seem light when we shelter behind what he has said. There may be a promise in Scripture that exactly fits your case, but if you do not look for it, you miss its comfort — like a prisoner holding a ring of keys, one of which would open the door. You remember the sayings of great writers; store your memory just as richly with the promises of God, so you can reach them when a doubt needs answering.
today: Pick one promise of Scripture and memorise it today — this verse will do — so it is on hand the next time fear speaks first.

context: Israel is in the wilderness, and God feeds them with manna. Each morning every household gathers just what it needs for that day. Whatever is hoarded spoils, and when the sun grows hot, what is left on the ground melts away.
reflection: Maintain a lively sense of your dependence on the Lord for the continuance of everything you enjoy. Do not try to live on old manna; yesterday's supply will not feed today. Fresh strength must come each morning, or the day outruns you. You may stand on the mountain today, but the one who set you there must keep you there. This hourly dependence the Lord means us to feel, for he teaches us to pray for daily bread and promises that as our days, so shall our strength be. Is it not good that it is so, since it brings us back to him often, and reminds us constantly of his unwearied love?
today: Ask God for what today actually requires — this meeting, this errand, this conversation — rather than for a stockpile against every imagined tomorrow.

context: On the night before his death, Jesus finishes preparing his disciples for what is coming. He is honest with them: in the world they will have trouble. But he tells them to take heart, because he has overcome the world, and in him they will have peace.
reflection: Christ never hid the cost from his followers; he promised trouble plainly. You are a traveller passing through country that is not yet home, and the road will have its ambushes; expect difficulty, and it will not take you by surprise. But expecting trouble is not the same as despairing over it. The word standing beside 'you shall have trouble' is 'be of good cheer, I have overcome.' God is with you to help and to strengthen you; he has said, 'I will be with you in trouble, and I will deliver you.' So face the day soberly but not fearfully — the outcome of the war is already settled.
today: Expect one difficulty today without being surprised or resentful when it arrives. Meet it as something already overcome.

context: The Song of Solomon is a poem of love between bride and bridegroom. Here the bride recalls her beloved's voice calling her at winter's end: the rains are over, flowers appear, and he invites her to rise up and come away with him.
reflection: I hear the voice of my Beloved, and he bids me rise. Well he may, for I have lain long enough among lower loves, desires, and pursuits. He calls me by the name 'my love' and counts me fair — reason enough for rising. 'Come away' has no harsh sound in it, for what is there to hold me? To come to him is to come home from exile, to come to land out of the storm, to come to rest after long labour. Lord, I would come, but I am caught among the thorns; raise me, draw me. Your grace can do it, and I will keep rising until I come away.
today: Identify one lower pursuit that has held your attention lately, and set it down for a day. Rising starts small.

context: Peter writes to believers scattered and under strain across Asia Minor. Having told them to humble themselves under God's hand, he gives the reason humility can rest easy: they may cast every anxiety on God, because he cares for them.
reflection: It soothes sorrow to feel that he cares for me. Do not always wear a brow of care; come, cast your burden on your Lord. You are staggering beneath a weight your Father would not feel; what crushes you is to him as the small dust of the balance. He who feeds the sparrows will furnish what you need. His eye is on you, his heart is moved for your trouble, and his hand will bring the help required. What a serene and quiet life you might lead if you left providing to the God of providence. He has never refused to carry your burdens, and he has never fainted under their weight.
today: Notice each time you pick the same worry back up today, and set it down again. The repetition is the practice.

context: Psalm 103 opens with David preaching to himself. He commands his own soul to bless the Lord and not to forget his benefits — forgiveness, healing, redemption, and steady lovingkindness — before a single request is made.
reflection: It is profitable to trace the hand of God in the lives of the saints of old, but is it not better still to trace it in your own? Your history is as full of God, of his goodness and his faithfulness, as theirs. Have you had no deliverances, no answered requests, no seasons when you were made to lie down in green pastures and led beside still waters? The goodness of God has been the same to us as to those who went before. Review your own life, then, and weave his mercies into a song; take the pure gold of thankfulness and the jewels of praise, and give them back to him.
today: Before tonight, write down three specific things God has done in your own story — not in general, in yours.

context: Jesus has just thanked his Father for revealing truth to the childlike. Then he turns and gives an open invitation to the crowd: all who labour and are heavy laden may come to him, and he will give them rest.
reflection: The word at the heart of Christ's call is the gentle word 'come.' Law drives; grace draws. Jesus is the good shepherd going before his sheep, leading them onward with that one sweet word. As a mother holds out her hand to her small child and woos it to walk by saying 'come,' even so does he. He goes ahead of you to clear the path, and all through life you hear his voice calling you after him. And in time the cry reverses: 'come' becomes your word to him — come, Lord, and abide with me; occupy the throne of my heart, and keep it.
today: Bring today's heaviest thing to Christ first, before you carry it to anyone else.

context: Zechariah speaks of a day known only to the Lord, unlike any other day — neither plain daylight nor night. Its surprise comes at the close: instead of deepening darkness, at evening time there shall be light.
reflection: We often look toward old age with foreboding, forgetting that at evening time it shall be light. For many saints, old age is the choicest season of life. A calmer air fans the traveller's cheek as the journey nears its end; fewer waves ruffle the sea, and a deep quiet reigns. The flash of youth's fire goes from the altar, but the more real flame of earnest feeling remains. The setting sun looks larger than it did at noon, and a splendour of glory tinges all the clouds around its going down. Ripe fruit of long experience is gathered as the rare repast of life's evening, and strength made perfect in weakness bears up with patience.
today: Ask someone a generation older than you what God has taught them. Evening light is meant to be shared.

context: Jeremiah is confined in the court of the guard while Jerusalem is under siege. In that narrow place the word of the Lord comes to him a second time: call to me, and I will answer, and show you great things you do not know.
reflection: One old version renders it 'great and reserved things.' There are reaches of the knowledge of God that are not stumbled into; they are climbed to by prayer. The common comforts of faith are shared by the whole family, but there is a nearer communion with God that most of us have only visited. God alone can carry us there, and persistent prayer is the chariot. If you would rise above ordinary, grovelling experience, look to the Rock that is higher than you, and gaze through the window of importunate prayer. When you open the window on your side, it will not be bolted on the other.
today: Pray today for something larger than your usual requests — a great thing — and keep asking through the week.

context: Paul reminds the Thessalonian believers that they are children of the day, not of the night. Because of that, he writes, let us not sleep as others do, but let us watch and be sober.
reflection: There are many ways of keeping spiritually awake, and one of the best is company. In Bunyan's story, the two pilgrims agreed that to prevent drowsiness on the road they would fall into good conversation, beginning where God began with them. Christians who isolate themselves and walk alone are very liable to grow drowsy; hold Christian company and you will be kept wakeful, refreshed, and encouraged to quicker progress. But as you take sweet counsel together, let the theme be worth staying awake for: let your heart be full of the Lord Jesus and your lips ready to speak of his worth. Watchfulness, it turns out, is not a solitary discipline.
today: Reach out to one believing friend today and talk about something real — where God began with you is a fine opening.

context: Psalm 84 is a pilgrim song of the sons of Korah, sung by travellers longing for God's house. Passing through the dry valley of Baca, the pilgrims dig wells there, and the rain fills the pools behind them for those who follow.
reflection: The comfort one traveller finds often proves serviceable to another, as wells dug by one company refresh those who come after. We read some word full of consolation and think: a brother has been here before us, and dug this well for himself, but also for us. The psalms are full of such waymarks through the vale of tears. And notice how the well fills — from the top, not from the bottom. We use the means; heaven sends the rain. We dig, but the blessing does not spring from the digging. Labour is not lost, yet it never replaces divine help. May the wells you dig in your own hard valleys hold water for someone else.
today: Share one thing that genuinely comforted you — a verse, a line, a practice — with someone walking a valley you have already crossed.

context: In the Sermon on the Mount, Jesus teaches his disciples how to pray, warning first against empty phrases and prayer for show. The pattern he gives begins not with requests but with relationship and reverence: Our Father in heaven, hallowed be your name.
reflection: This prayer begins where all true prayer must begin, with the childlike cry, 'our Father.' There is no praying rightly until we can say, 'I will arise and go to my Father.' The child who lisps 'Abba' soon perceives the Father's grandeur in heaven, and worship rises: hallowed be your name. From there the prayer walks down into ordinary need — give us this day our daily bread — and into honest confession — forgive us our debts, as we forgive our debtors. From the simple sense of being a child, up to fellowship with the reigning Lord, this short model conducts the soul. Lord, teach us to pray like this.
today: Pray the Lord's Prayer slowly today, one line at a time, letting each phrase finish before the next begins.

context: Ecclesiastes gathers the reflections of the Teacher on life's shape and seasons. Here he observes that the end of a thing is better than its beginning, and that a patient spirit outweighs a proud one.
reflection: See that creeping worm, how contemptible its appearance — that is the beginning of a thing. Now mark the winged creature playing in the sunbeams, sipping at the flower bells — that is the end of it. Or watch the rough diamond set on the cutter's wheel: it loses much, much that seemed costly to itself, yet every cut prepares the ray that will one day flash from the finished stone. You are in the time of the cutting process. Do not judge the work by the middle of it. Let faith and patience have their perfect work, for the end of a thing is better than its beginning, and the patient in spirit than the proud.
today: Whatever feels half-finished or diminished in your life right now, name it in process rather than ruined, and keep going one more day.

context: Psalm 45 is a wedding song addressed to the king, celebrating his beauty, grace, and blessing. Christians from the earliest days have heard in its praise a portrait of Christ, the King whose lips are full of grace.
reflection: The whole person of Jesus is as one gem, one complete impression of the seal. His character is not a mass of fair colours mixed confusedly, nor a heap of precious stones piled carelessly one upon another; every feature is in its proper place, and each adorns the others. No single quality draws attention at the expense of the rest — power, grace, justice, tenderness, truth, and majesty meet in him as all colours blend into one rainbow. He is music without discord. If all the virtues of the most excellent people were bound in one bundle, they could not rival him. He is the mirror of all perfection.
today: Read Psalm 45 today and spend one unhurried minute simply admiring Christ, asking for nothing.

context: Hosea's final chapter calls wayward Israel home. God answers the returning people with a promise beyond what they asked: I will heal their backsliding, I will love them freely, for my anger is turned away.
reflection: The whole sentence hinges on the word 'freely.' This is how love streams from heaven to earth — spontaneous, flowing toward those who neither deserved it, purchased it, nor sought it. We keep raising objections. Lord, my heart is so hard. 'I will love them freely.' But I do not feel my need as I ought. 'I will love them freely.' His love does not wait for you to become fit for it; that would not be free love at all. And this word was written specially for those who have wandered: I will heal their backsliding. Surely the generosity of the promise is enough to break a heart open, and to bring it home.
today: If you have been keeping distance from God until you feel readier, return today as you are. The love is free or it is not love.

context: Paul writes to the Philippians from prison, thanking them for their gift. He is careful to say his gratitude is not neediness: he has learned, in whatever state he is, to be content.
reflection: Contentment is not a natural growth. Weeds spring up on their own — covetousness and murmuring need no sowing — but the precious things must be cultivated. If we would have wheat, we must plough and sow; if we want flowers, there must be a garden and a gardener's care. Notice that Paul says, 'I have learned to be content,' which is as much as to say he once did not know how. It cost him some pains to reach that mystery; no doubt he sometimes thought he had learned it, and then broke down. Do not imagine contentment comes without discipline. It is not a mood that arrives; it is a science acquired gradually.
today: Catch one complaint today before it leaves your mouth, and replace it with a specific thanks. That is one lesson in the course.

context: In Psalm 52 David answers the treachery of Doeg, a powerful man who used lies to destroy. Against that picture David sets himself: a green olive tree in the house of God, trusting in the mercy of God for ever and ever.
reflection: Meditate a little on this mercy of the Lord. It is tender mercy: with gentle touch he heals the broken in heart and binds up their wounds. It is great mercy: there is nothing little in God, and his mercy is like himself. It is undeserved mercy, as all true mercy must be, for deserved mercy is only another name for justice. It is rich mercy — a cordial to drooping spirits, a bandage for broken bones. It is abounding mercy: millions have received it, yet it remains as fresh, as full, and as free as ever. And it is unfailing mercy: it will be with you living, and with you to the end.
today: Choose one of those words — tender, great, undeserved, rich, abounding, unfailing — and watch for that mercy in your day.

context: The Pharisees have begun plotting against Jesus, and he withdraws. Even in retreat, great crowds follow him — the sick in all their variety — and Matthew records the quiet, sweeping summary: he healed them all.
reflection: What a mass of misery must have passed before the eye of Jesus, yet we never read that he was disgusted; he waited patiently on every case. Whatever new shape the trouble took, he was ready for it, and master of it. Fever or palsy, blindness or madness — all knew the power of his word. It is even so this morning. Whatever my own case may be, the beloved Physician can meet it; and whatever the state of those I remember in prayer, I may have hope for each of them, for all of them, when I remember him. He who once walked the hospitals still works among the sons of men.
today: Pray by name for one person whose trouble seems beyond fixing, remembering that no case was ever too hard for him.

context: The last verses of 2 Kings follow Jehoiachin, the exiled king of Judah, released from a Babylonian prison after thirty-seven years. He is given a place at the king's table and a daily allowance, every day, all the days of his life.
reflection: Jehoiachin was not handed a store to last for months; his provision came as a daily portion, and in this he pictures us well. A daily portion is all a person really needs. We do not need tomorrow's supplies, for tomorrow's wants are as yet unborn; the thirst of June need not be quenched in February. Enough is not only as good as a feast — it is all that anyone can truly enjoy. One staff helps a traveller; a bundle of staffs is a burden. In matters of grace it is the same: you hold no reserve of strength, and day by day you draw fresh help from above. The allowance is continual. It will be there tomorrow.
today: Take today's provision as today's, without auditing whether it will stretch to cover next month. Tomorrow has its own allowance.

context: Psalm 37 is David's counsel against fretting over wrongdoers who seem to prosper. In place of envy he sets a different occupation: delight yourself in the Lord, and he shall give you the desires of your heart.
reflection: The life of the believer is described here as delight in God — evidence that true faith overflows with happiness. To many people religion looks like duty, service, or necessity, never pleasure; for them no two words stand further apart than holiness and joy. But those who know God find that his ways are ways of pleasantness. We do not serve him from compulsion; our faith is no fetter, and no one drags us to what we love. Delight and true devotion are allied as root and flower. Where delight in God grows, desire itself is slowly retuned, until the things we want most are the very things he loves to give.
today: Do one thing with God today for the pleasure of it — a walk, a psalm, music — not because it is required.

context: Writing to the Ephesians about the church's maturity, Paul describes a body being built up in love. Its members, speaking the truth in love, are to grow up in every way into Christ, who is the head.
reflection: Many believers remain the same size year after year, stunted in spiritual things; they exist, but they do not grow up into him in all things. Why rest content in the green blade when you might advance to the ear, and ripen into full corn? It is well to believe and to know you are held, but not to stop wishing to know more of the fulness that is in Christ. If we would ripen in grace, we must live near to Jesus, as fruit ripens in sunshine — in his presence, in unhurried communion with him. Growth is never hurried, but it is never accidental either. Come near, and stay near.
today: Pick one small habit of nearness — a psalm at lunch, prayer on the commute — and begin it today rather than someday.

context: Colossians opens with Paul thanking God for a church he has never visited. He traces their faith in Christ and their love for all the saints back to a source: the hope laid up for them in heaven, which they heard in the gospel.
reflection: Our hope for the future is the mainspring of our joy here. To the weary and toil-worn, the word 'rest' is full of heaven, and all that we can rightly desire is promised there. But let it never be said of us that we dream about the future and forget the present; let the future sanctify the present to its highest uses. The one who carries this hope goes about ordinary work with vigour, for the joy of the Lord is his strength. He can labour without immediate reward, because he is not working for applause that has to arrive by Friday. Hope is not an escape from today's tasks; it is the strength for them.
today: Do today's least-rewarding task well, as work that counts beyond this week's recognition.

context: Psalm 119 is a long meditation on the word of God, verse after verse of resolve and request. Here the psalmist states a simple intention: I will meditate on your precepts, and have respect to your ways.
reflection: There are times when solitude is better than society, and silence wiser than speech. We would be stronger if we were more often alone with God, gathering through meditation the real nutriment of his word. Truth is like the cluster of the vine: if we would have wine from it, we must bruise it, pressing it many times. The body is not fed by putting food in the mouth but by digestion, and the soul is not nourished by merely hearing truth but by dwelling on it. Some hear much and advance little, because they love the wheat but do not grind it; the water flows at their feet, but they will not stoop to drink.
today: Take one verse — this one works — and return to it three times today: morning, midday, evening. Let it be ground, not just glanced at.

context: Paul urges the Philippian believers to live without grumbling or disputing, so that they may be blameless and sincere. In the midst of a crooked generation, he says, they shine as lights in the world.
reflection: Lights make things visible. A Christian should so shine that a person could not live beside him for a week without noticing what he lives by; the image of Jesus should be legible in his daily actions. Lights guide: we help those around us who are in the dark, and point the weary to a resting place. Lights warn, marking the rocks for those still out on the water. And lights cheer. A Christian ought to be a comforter, with kind words on his lips and sympathy in his heart, carrying brightness wherever he goes. None of this is performed for an audience; a lamp does not announce itself. It simply burns where it is set.
today: Be light in the room you are already in: one kind word, one honest answer, one act of quiet help.

context: Paul reminds Titus what believers were before grace found them — foolish, disobedient, led astray. Then comes the turn: but when the kindness and love of God our Saviour toward man appeared, he saved us, not by our works but by his mercy.
reflection: Let the mind consider the history of the Redeemer's love, and a thousand acts of affection suggest themselves, all designed to weave the heart into Christ. When we meditate on this love, and see him endowing his people with all his wealth, the soul may well faint for joy. Even the partial sense of it we are given is more than the heart can hold; what must a complete view be? By faith we see, as in a glass darkly, the reflection of unbounded treasure. Until we see clearly, our warmest praise belongs to our loving benefactor, Jesus Christ our Lord, whose kindness appeared not because we were ready, but because he is kind.
today: Thank God today for one kindness you never asked for and never earned. Be specific.

context: The Galatian churches were being pressured to add law-keeping to faith in Christ. Paul answers with a charge: stand fast in the liberty with which Christ has made us free, and do not be entangled again in a yoke of bondage.
reflection: This liberty makes you free to heaven's charter, the Bible. Here is a choice passage: 'When you pass through the rivers, I will be with you' — you are free to that. 'My kindness shall not depart from you' — you are free to that also. You are a welcome guest at the table of the promises, a treasury filled with boundless stores of grace, and not one promise will be withheld. You are free, too, to the throne of grace: whatever your desires, your difficulties, your wants, you are at liberty to spread them all before him, at any hour — midnight's darkest or noon's most burning. Exercise your right, and live up to your privilege.
today: Approach God today without first rehearsing your qualifications. The door is already open; use it.

context: Lamentations is Jeremiah's grief over ruined Jerusalem, and this line rises from its centre, just after the memory that God's mercies are new every morning. With everything else lost, the poet says: the Lord is my portion; therefore I will hope in him.
reflection: It is not 'the Lord is partly my portion,' nor 'the Lord is in my portion'; he himself makes up the whole of the soul's inheritance. Not his gifts merely, nor his blessings, but God himself. It is not easy to satisfy human desire; whenever we dream we are satisfied, we wake to find something still beyond, and the heart cries, give, give. But all we can truly wish for is found in our divine portion, so that we say, 'Whom have I in heaven but you, and there is none on earth I desire besides you.' The writer had lost nearly everything else when he said it — and found the portion still intact.
today: When something is taken from you today, even something small, note what remains: the portion no loss can touch.

context: Paul writes to the church in Rome about Abraham, who was promised a son though he and Sarah were old. Abraham did not stagger at the promise through unbelief but was strong in faith, giving glory to God.
reflection: Take good care of your faith, for faith is the channel by which blessing travels between your soul and God. Prayer cannot bring down answers from his throne unless it is the earnest prayer of one who believes. Faith is the road on which the horses of prayer may travel, even in the deepest winter; but blockade that road, and how can we speak with the great King? Faith links you with God himself; it engages his strength on your side and helps you stand when everything else gives way. Am I in trouble? I can find help by faith. Am I pressed hard? My soul leans on her dear refuge by faith.
today: Before the day starts, name one promise of God you actually believe, and let it carry one worry.

context: Paul instructs Timothy, his young colleague in Ephesus, on what to say to the rich: not to trust in uncertain riches but in the living God, who gives us richly all things to enjoy.
reflection: Our Lord is always giving, and does not for a moment withdraw his hand. As long as a vessel of grace is not yet full to the brim, the oil does not stop. He is a sun always shining; he is manna always falling around the camp; the rain of his grace is always dropping; the river of his bounty is always flowing, and the well-spring of his love constantly overflows. Daily we pluck his fruit, and daily his branches bend down to our hand with a fresh store of mercy. Who has ever returned from his door unblessed? His mercies are new every morning and fresh every evening. Who can count the number of his benefits?
today: Count three ordinary gifts you will use today, such as food, light, a friend, and thank the giver for each by name.

context: Paul tells the Corinthians how he arrived in Macedonia worn down, troubled on every side, with fightings without and fears within. God, who comforts those who are cast down, comforted him by the arrival of his friend Titus.
reflection: Who comforts like God? Go to some distressed child of God and whisper sweet promises in his ear; he hardly hears the voice of the charmer. But let God come to his child, let God lift up his countenance, and the mourner's eyes glisten with hope. There is no balm in Gilead, but there is balm in God. One word from God is like a piece of gold, and the Christian is the goldbeater who can hammer that promise out for whole weeks. So do not sit down in despair. When you are a dry well, do not first run to earthly acquaintances; go first to the Comforter, and ask him for consolation.
today: When the low mood comes today, take it to God directly before taking it to anyone else.

context: Closing his letter to the Ephesians, Paul has described the armour of God. He now adds the posture that holds it all together: praying always, with all prayer and supplication, watching with perseverance for all the saints.
reflection: What multitudes of prayers we have offered since we first learned to pray. We have asked for mercy, for grace to hold us and grace to restrain us, for deliverance in temptation, for help in duty, for strength in trial. We have come to God as constant beggars, asking for everything; and bear witness, you have never been able to get anything for your soul elsewhere. All the bread your soul has eaten has come down from heaven. Your soul has never grown rich in itself; it has always been a pensioner on the daily kindness of God. As your prayers have been many, so also have been his answers.
today: Keep the line open all day: a sentence of prayer before each task, not just one block in the morning.

context: Through Isaiah, God promises a renewed Jerusalem in which he himself will rejoice over his people. The voice of weeping will be no more heard in her, nor the voice of crying.
reflection: A day is coming when God's people will weep no more, for the outward causes of grief will be gone. No broken friendships, no blighted prospects; no pain, no bereavement, no fear of change. They dwell in a city that cannot be stormed; they bask in a sun that never sets; they drink of a river that never runs dry; they pluck fruit from a tree that never withers. Well may they cease to mourn who have ceased to sin. This is not a dream to escape into but a promise to lean on: the tears of the present are real, and so is the God who has appointed their end.
today: Hold today's griefs honestly but loosely; they are real, and they are not permanent.

context: Paul writes to the Philippians from prison, thanking them for their gift. He has learned contentment in every state: he knows how to be abased, and he knows how to abound.
reflection: Many know how to be abased who have not learned how to abound. Set on top of a pinnacle, their heads grow dizzy and they are ready to fall. The crucible of adversity is often a less severe trial than the refining pot of prosperity: we are full, and we forget God. Yet it need not be so, for Paul knew how to use much when he had much. Abundant grace enabled him to bear abundant prosperity; when he had a full sail he carried much ballast, and so floated safely. It takes more than human skill to carry the brimming cup of joy with a steady hand, but that skill can be learned, and grace teaches it.
today: If today goes well, treat it as an exercise of the same trust you would need on a hard day.

context: Paul prays for the believers in Ephesus that, rooted and grounded in love, they may grasp the breadth and length and depth and height of the love of Christ, a love that passes knowledge.
reflection: The love of Christ in its sweetness, its fullness, its greatness, its faithfulness, passes all human comprehension. Where shall language be found to describe it? All descriptive words merely touch the surface, as the swallow skims the water but does not dive into its depths; measureless deeps lie beneath. To take any right measure of this love you must weigh both his height and his descent: he reigned above all creatures, surrounded by the praises of heaven, and yet he stooped to be a man, and a man of sorrows, and to endure shame and death. That distance, from the highest throne to the lowest grief, is the measure of a love that cannot be measured.
today: You will not reach the bottom of Christ's love today; go one thought deeper into it anyway.

context: In Psalm 29 David hears the voice of the Lord in a great storm and calls the hosts of heaven to respond: give unto the Lord the glory due unto his name; worship the Lord in the beauty of holiness.
reflection: God is glorious in his character, and glorious in all that flows from it; the glory belongs to him alone. And there is nothing in ourselves in which to glory, for what do we have that we did not receive? The moment we glorify ourselves we set ourselves up as rivals to the Most High. Shall the insect of an hour glorify itself against the sun that warmed it into life? Shall the clay exalt itself above the potter who shaped it on the wheel? Perhaps one of the hardest lessons of the Christian life is the sentence, not unto us, but unto your name be glory. It is a lesson God keeps teaching, and it is worth the learning.
today: When something goes well today, pass the credit along: inwardly to God, outwardly to whoever helped.

context: Jeremiah, prophesying to a Judah failed by corrupt kings, announces a righteous Branch to be raised up from David's line, a king who will reign wisely, and whose name is The Lord Our Righteousness.
reflection: It will always give a Christian the greatest calm and peace to think of the perfect righteousness of Christ. How often God's people are downcast and sad, and it is no wonder when they dwell only on the corruption of their own hearts. That corruption is real, but why not go a little further and remember that Christ is made unto us righteousness? On the cross he said, it is finished; and what is finished does not depend on today's performance. Nothing is lacking in the Lord. Nor does resting there make anyone careless: you will not find a holier people than those who receive Christ's righteousness with gratitude.
today: When your failures replay today, answer them with the words it is finished, not with a tally of your record.

context: Near the close of his letter to the Colossians, Paul turns to brief final instructions. The first of them is this: continue in prayer, and watch in the same with thanksgiving.
reflection: It is striking how large a portion of Scripture is occupied with prayer, whether in examples, precepts, or promises. We scarcely open the Bible before men begin to call on the name of the Lord, and the book closes with an earnest amen. Here is wrestling Jacob, there Daniel who prayed three times a day, and David calling on God with his whole heart; on the mountain Elijah, in the dungeon Paul and Silas. Whatever God has made prominent in his word, he intended to be prominent in our lives. If he has said much about prayer, it is because he knows we have much need of it. So deep are our needs that we must not cease to pray.
today: Pick a fixed moment, such as the kettle boiling or the commute, and let it be a daily trigger to pray.

context: David closes Psalm 138 with confidence in God's ongoing work: the Lord will perfect that which concerns me; your mercy, O Lord, endures forever. He asks God not to forsake the works of his own hands.
reflection: The psalmist's confidence was not in himself. He did not say, my faith is so steady it cannot stagger, my love so warm it will never grow cold, my resolution so firm that nothing can move it. His dependence was on the Lord alone. It is the Lord who began the good work in us; it is he who has carried it on; and if he does not finish it, it never will be complete. Our confidence must rest not in what we have done, nor in what we have resolved to do, but in what the Lord will do. Unbelief whispers, you will never be able to stand. Alone, true enough. But the one who began will finish.
today: Where you feel half-finished today, remember whose workmanship you are, and leave the completing to him.

context: God speaks through Jeremiah to Israel in exile, a people who felt cast off: I have loved you with an everlasting love; therefore with lovingkindness have I drawn you.
reflection: The thunders of the law may drive us toward Christ, but the final victory is won by lovingkindness. The prodigal set out for his father's house from a sense of need, but his father saw him a great way off and ran to him, so the last steps he took were with the kiss still warm upon his cheek and the welcome musical in his ears. The Master knocked once with the iron hand of the law, and the door was barricaded shut. He came again and knocked softly with the hand that bears the print of the nail, and the door opened. So it is in every case: what fear could never do, kindness does. Lovingkindness wins the day.
today: Since kindness is how God won you, try kindness before argument with the difficult person in your day.

context: After his resurrection, Jesus stands on the shore of the Sea of Tiberias while his disciples fish through a fruitless night. He directs their catch, then welcomes them to a fire with bread and fish: come and dine.
reflection: Come and dine invites the believer to a holy nearness to Jesus: the same table, the same food, sometimes leaning our head upon his shoulder. It is also an invitation to fellowship with the saints. Christians may differ on a variety of points, but they have one spiritual appetite, and if we cannot all feel alike, we can all feed alike on the bread of life. The nearer we come to Jesus, the nearer we find ourselves to one another. And it is the source of strength: to look at Christ is to live, but for strength to serve him you must come and dine. Much of our weakness comes from neglecting this invitation.
today: Do not run on empty for Christ; sit down with him first. A passage read slowly counts as a meal.

context: With Jerusalem under threat of invasion, Isaiah brings King Ahaz a sign from God: a virgin shall conceive and bear a son, and shall call his name Immanuel, God with us. The gospels see this fulfilled in the birth of Jesus.
reflection: Go down to Bethlehem in company with wondering shepherds and adoring wise men, and see him who was born King. Jesus is God come near, our Lord and our God, and yet our brother and friend. Notice his humble arrival: his mother a village girl rather than a princess, the man she was engaged to poor, and the lodging afforded the newborn King a stable. Then notice what the name carries. Immanuel: God with us in our nature, in our sorrow, in our life's work, in our grave; and now, rather, we with him. There is no place an ordinary life can go where that name does not already stand.
today: Carry the single word Immanuel through the day; whatever the hour holds, God is with us in it.

context: Psalm 67 asks God's blessing so that his way may be known on the earth. It closes with harvest gratitude: then shall the earth yield her increase, and God, even our own God, shall bless us.
reflection: It is strange how little use we make of the blessings God gives us, but stranger still how little use we make of God himself. Though he is our own God, we seldom ask counsel of him, and go about our business without seeking his guidance; in trouble we strain to carry our own burdens instead of casting them on him. Yet he seems to say: I am yours, soul; come and make use of me as you will; come freely to my store, and the more often, the more welcome. Never be in want while you have a God to go to; never faint while you have God to help you. Make use of your God.
today: Take one practical decision to God today before you take it to a search bar or a friend.

context: Paul warns the Colossians against being carried off by hollow philosophy. His answer is Christ himself: in him dwells all the fullness of the Godhead bodily, and you are complete in him.
reflection: All the fullness that dwells in Christ is set toward our good. His power is our protection, his wisdom our direction, his knowledge our instruction, his love our comfort, his mercy our solace, and his unchangeableness our trust. He makes no reserve, but opens his storehouses and bids us take. The fathomless love of the Saviour's heart is every drop of it ours. Rise, believer, and behold the Lord Jesus putting the whole of himself to the work of keeping you. You are not complete in your circumstances, your achievements, or your reputation, and you were never meant to be; you are complete in him, and he is sufficient for whatever you lack.
today: Where you feel lacking today, finish the sentence honestly: but I am complete in him.

context: Paul lists the hard things that press on believers, including trouble, distress, persecution and peril, and concludes that in all these things we are more than conquerors through him that loved us.
reflection: We go to Christ for forgiveness, and then too often look to willpower alone for strength to fight our sins. Take your struggles to the cross instead, for that is where the old self is dealt with. Do you want to overcome an angry temper? It is very possible you have never tried the right way of going to Jesus with it. How did you first find mercy? You came to him just as you were and trusted him. Deal with the temper in the same way: Lord, I trust you to deliver me from it. Does the world entangle you? Struggle as long as you please; the besetting sin yields at the cross, not before it.
today: Name your most stubborn fault to Jesus in plain words today, and ask him, not just yourself, to deal with it.

context: Psalm 66 summons all lands to make a joyful noise to God: sing forth the honour of his name, make his praise glorious. The psalmist then recounts what God has done for his people, and for his own soul.
reflection: Praise is God's rightful due, and for the one who has received his grace it is also the natural fruit of the life he has given. The law written on the heart teaches us that it is right to praise him; you are blessed in order that you may bless him. So do not let your harp hang on the willows; take it down and, with a grateful heart, bring out its music. With every morning's dawn lift up your notes of thanksgiving, and let every setting sun be followed by your song. Girdle the day with praise: it began in his mercy, it is carried by his kindness, and it can close in his honour.
today: Bookend the day: one sentence of thanks when you wake, one before you sleep.

context: Elijah, exhausted and fleeing for his life, sleeps under a juniper tree. An angel wakes him with baked bread and water, and in the strength of that food he travels forty days and nights to Horeb, the mount of God.
reflection: All the strength God supplies is meant for service, not for ease or boasting. Elijah was not fed under the juniper tree so that he could stretch out at leisure; he was fed for a forty-day journey. When the Master invited his disciples to come and dine, he followed the meal with feed my sheep. So it is with us: we eat the bread of heaven that we may spend our strength in the Master's work. Some are content to live on Christ who are less eager to live for him. Do not hold the grain of truth as the mummy's hand held wheat for ages, never letting it grow; sow it and water it.
today: Whatever renewed you recently, whether rest, encouragement or answered prayer, spend some of that strength on someone else today.

context: Jeremiah laments over ruined Jerusalem, yet in the middle of the book calls the people back to God: let us search and try our ways, and lift up our heart with our hands unto God in the heavens.
reflection: Prayer, apart from any answer it brings, is itself a great benefit. A true prayer is an inventory of needs, a revelation of hidden poverty, and that is a healthy discovery for beings as proud as we are. The healthiest state of a Christian is to be always empty in self and constantly depending on the Lord for supplies; poor in self and rich in Jesus. And prayer strengthens: as the runner gains power for the race by daily exercise, so we acquire energy for the great race of life by the hallowed labour of prayer. It girds human weakness with divine strength, and lays the heart where it should be, lifted up to God and low before him.
today: Pray one honest inventory today: not polished words, just the actual list of what you need.

context: Psalm 74 laments the destruction of the sanctuary and asks how long the enemy will mock. Yet Asaph steadies himself on God's rule over creation: you have set all the borders of the earth; you have made summer and winter.
reflection: Winter in the soul is not a comfortable season, and if it is upon you now it is painful. But there is this comfort: the Lord makes it. He who keeps his covenant with day and night will not prove unfaithful to you. Losses, crosses, heaviness and sickness come with wise design; frosts kill noxious insects and break up the hard clods of the field. May such good results follow our winters of affliction. And notice how we prize the fire in cold weather. Prize your Lord in the same way, the constant source of warmth and comfort in every time of trouble; draw near to him, and wrap yourself in the warm garments of his promises.
today: If you are in a wintry stretch, do the winter things: stay near the fire, keep the promises close, wait for spring.

context: On the night before his death, Jesus prays for his disciples, not that they be taken out of the world but kept within it. He asks the Father: sanctify them through your truth; your word is truth.
reflection: Growth in holiness is God's work in us, but there is a visible instrument in his hand which must not be forgotten: sanctify them through your truth; your word is truth. The Spirit brings the teachings of Scripture to mind and applies them with power; heard in the ear and received in the heart, they work in us to will and to do of God's good pleasure. The truth is the sanctifier: if we do not hear or read it, we should not expect to grow. We only progress in sound living as we progress in sound understanding. Your word is a lamp to my feet, and a light to my path.
today: Read a short passage slowly and carry one line of it into your work; that is how the word does its work.

context: John writes to believers so that they may know they abide in Christ. The test he gives is practical: he that says he abides in him ought himself also so to walk, even as he walked.
reflection: Why should Christians imitate Christ? For their own sake first: if they would know the vigour of growing grace and happy communion with Jesus, let him be their model. Nothing helps you walk toward heaven with good speed like wearing the image of Jesus on your heart to rule all its motions; Peter following afar off was both unsafe and uneasy. And imitate him for his own sake. Do you love your Saviour? Is his name precious to you? Then let the world see him in you. The clearest argument for the gospel remains a life shaped like his: patient under wrong, quick to serve, honest, and unhurried in kindness.
today: Choose one situation today, a queue, an email, an interruption, and handle it the way Jesus would have.

context: Psalm 107 tells of the redeemed who wandered in the wilderness, hungry and thirsty, and cried to the Lord in their trouble. He led them forth by the right way, that they might go to a city of habitation.
reflection: Changing experience often makes the anxious believer ask, why is it this way with me? I looked for light, and darkness came; for peace, and trouble arrived. Yesterday I could see the whole landscape clearly; today my hopes are clouded. Can this be the way God brings his people home? Yes, it is even so. The eclipse of your faith, the dimness of your hope, these are among the means by which you are ripened for what lies ahead. Trials test and strengthen faith; they are waves that wash you further upon the rock. The road bends, but it is still the right way, and it still ends at home.
today: Treat today's detour as road rather than wilderness; keep walking, and keep talking to the guide.

context: On the last and greatest day of the Feast of Tabernacles in Jerusalem, Jesus stood and cried out to the crowds: if any man thirst, let him come unto me and drink.
reflection: Notice how open the invitation is. Provision is made most plentifully: peace for the conscience, instruction for the understanding, and the person of Jesus for the heart's affection. Thirst is terrible, but Jesus can remove it; though the soul were utterly famished, he could restore it. And the proclamation is free: every thirsty one is welcome, and no qualification is asked but thirst itself. Whether it is the thirst left behind by ambition, pleasure, achievement or restlessness, whoever suffers from it is invited. His patience marked the whole feast; to the last day he stood and pleaded, and he pleads still. All you must bring to this fountain is the thirst.
today: Bring the actual thirst, the restlessness underneath today's wants, to Christ instead of to the usual substitutes.

context: In his final song before Israel enters the land, Moses rehearses God's dealings with his people. Among all the nations of the earth, the Lord's portion is his people; Jacob is the lot of his inheritance.
reflection: Of all that God possesses, the heavens, the earth, the countless worlds, he calls his people his portion. They are his by love, and his at great cost: redeemed not with corruptible things such as silver and gold, but with the precious blood of Christ. About his title there can be no dispute; he forgets none of those he has redeemed, and remembers well those for whom he gave himself. And they are his by a long, patient winning: how long he laid siege to our barred hearts before they opened to him. If God counts you part of his treasure, your worth does not rise and fall with your week.
today: Live today as someone treasured: nothing you own or achieve makes God's claim on you greater or smaller.

context: In Psalm 73 Asaph confesses that envy of the wicked had made him bitter, foolish and ignorant before God. Then comes the turn: nevertheless I am continually with you; you have held me by my right hand.
reflection: Nevertheless. As if, despite all the foolishness he had just confessed, it remained no less true that he belonged to God. Fully conscious of his failings, by an outburst of faith he sings, nevertheless I am continually with you. Continually on his mind: God is always thinking of you for your good. Continually before his eye: the eye of the Lord never sleeps, but perpetually watches over your welfare. Continually in his hand, so that none can pluck you from it. Continually on his heart, worn there as a memorial, as the high priest bore the names of the tribes over his heart. Learn to say it in your worst hour: nevertheless.
today: After your next failure today, say the word nevertheless before anything else, and finish Asaph's sentence.

context: In the closing lines of the Bible, an invitation goes out: the Spirit and the bride say, come. Let him that is athirst come; and whosoever will, let him take the water of life freely.
reflection: Jesus says, take freely. He wants no payment and no preparation, and seeks no recommendation from our feelings. No one stands at a public drinking fountain feeling for his purse, crying, I cannot drink because I have no money in my pocket. However poor the man, there is the fountain, and just as he is he may drink of it. Passers-by do not look for a warrant for drinking; the fountain being there is their warrant. The only ones who go thirsty past such a fountain are those too proud to stoop to it. Grace works the same way: the offer itself is your right to receive, and thirst is the only cup required.
today: Whatever you need from God today, come without tidying yourself up first; the offer says freely, as is.

context: Peter ends his second letter, and with it his written ministry, with a final charge to believers: grow in grace, and in the knowledge of our Lord and Saviour Jesus Christ.
reflection: Grow in grace: not in one grace only, but in all. Grow in faith; believe the promises more firmly than you have done. Grow in love; ask that it may be more extended, more intense, more practical, shaping every thought, word and deed. Grow in humility; seek to lie low and know more of your own nothingness, and as you grow downward, grow upward, drawing nearer to God in prayer and fellowship. And grow in the knowledge of Christ. Whoever has sipped this wine thirsts for more, for Christ satisfies in such a way that the appetite is whetted rather than cloyed. Love always cries, nearer, nearer.
today: Pick one grace, whether faith, love or humility, and give it one deliberate exercise today.

context: David writes Psalm 55 in anguish, betrayed by a close friend in a city full of strife. Out of that distress comes his counsel: cast your burden upon the Lord, and he shall sustain you.
reflection: Anxious care, carried far enough, quietly assumes that we are wiser than God. We take up our weary burden as if he were unable or unwilling to carry it for us, and we labour to remember what we imagine he will forget. Carrying it alone also leads us further astray: the one who cannot leave his affairs in God's hand is soon tempted to help himself by wrong means, going to the broken cistern instead of the fountain. There is a better way. Through simple trust in his promise, cast each burden on him as it comes, and find that he sustains both the load and the one who was carrying it.
today: Each time a worry returns today, hand it over again; casting is not one act but a habit.

context: The writer of Hebrews addresses Jewish Christians, drawing on Israel's wilderness journey and God's rest after creation. Though an earlier generation failed to enter Canaan's rest, he concludes that a deeper rest still remains open for the people of God.
reflection: Here you are born to toil and weariness. Eager to serve, you find your strength unequal to your zeal — not wearied of the labour, but wearied in it. Take heart: the hot day of weariness does not last for ever. Here, rest is partial; there, it is perfect. Your best joys now carry 'mortal' on their brow — fair flowers fade, pleasant days shade into night — but a rest remains that cannot be exhausted or taken away. The toil-worn labourer will one day rest for ever in the presence of God.
today: When tiredness sets in today, let it remind you that rest is promised, not earned; do your work, then stop without guilt.

context: Paul writes to the church in Corinth, where some were saying there is no resurrection of the dead. He answers that Christ has in fact been raised, the firstfruits of those who sleep, and that everything Christians hope for stands on this.
reflection: The whole of Christian faith rests on the fact that Christ is risen from the dead. If He is not risen, preaching is vain and faith is vain; but because He lives, nothing given to Him is lost. A silver thread of resurrection runs through all the believer's blessings and binds them together: forgiveness, new life, hope beyond the grave. This is not a theory to admire but ground to stand on. The tomb is empty; therefore your prayers, your labour, and your grief are not final things. Christ is risen, and everything else follows.
today: Face today's setbacks from the far side of the empty tomb: nothing entrusted to the risen Christ stays buried.

context: Psalm 38 is David's prayer in a season of illness, guilt, and hostile whispering. Worn down in body and conscience, he ends not with answers but with a plea: do not forsake me, O Lord, be not far from me.
reflection: We pray this in the hour of trial, but forget that we need it at all times. There is no moment, however holy, in which we can do without God's constant upholding. A child learning to walk still needs a steadying hand; a ship left by the pilot drifts at once. So pray it over everything: forsake me not in my joys, lest they absorb my heart; nor in my sorrows, lest I grumble against You. Without You I am weak; with You I am strong. As a hen covers her brood, cover me, and let me find refuge under Your wings.
today: Say 'forsake me not, O Lord' once in the morning and once at night — on an ordinary day, not just a hard one.

context: Through Ezekiel, God speaks to Israel in exile. After indicting leaders who fed themselves instead of the flock, He promises to shepherd His people Himself, to settle them in safety, and to send down showers of blessing in their season.
reflection: Only one voice can speak to the clouds and bid them bring rain. Grace is like that: it is God's to give, and it is needed — you may break the clods and sow the seed, but you cannot make it rain. And He does not promise drops but showers, given in season. Is this a season of drought for you? Then that is the season for showers. Are the clouds black and heavy? That too is the season for showers. As your days, so shall your strength be. Look up today, parched plant, and open your leaves for a heavenly watering.
today: Name the driest area of your life and ask God plainly for rain there, then leave the timing of the shower to Him.

context: This proverb belongs to a collection contrasting the generous and the grasping. Its promise is simple: the liberal soul is made fat, and the one who waters others will be watered himself — generosity comes back to the giver.
reflection: To get, we must give; to accumulate, scatter; to be made happy, make others happy. In watering others we are ourselves watered. Efforts to be useful draw out powers we did not know we had; we do not discover our own tenderness until we try to dry a widow's tears. We go to teach and come away taught. Like the two men in the snow — one rubbed the other's limbs to keep him alive, and in doing so kept his own blood moving. The widow of Sarepta fed the prophet from her scant store, and never again knew want. Give, and it shall be given to you.
today: Water someone today — a meal, an hour, an encouraging message — and notice how the watering refreshes you as well.

context: David closes Psalm 27, a psalm of confidence written under threat from enemies, by turning to instruct the listener: wait on the Lord, be of good courage, and He shall strengthen your heart.
reflection: Waiting may seem easy, but it is a posture a soldier learns only after years of training; marching is far easier than standing still. There are hours of perplexity when even the most willing spirit does not know what part to take. What then? Not despair, not retreat, not a rush forward in presumption — simply wait. Wait in prayer: spread the case before God and plead His promise of aid. Wait in faith: if He keeps you tarrying even till midnight, He will come at the right time. Wait in quiet patience, saying, not my will, but Yours be done.
today: Pick the decision you most want to force this week, and deliberately give it one more day of prayer before acting.

context: Job has lost children, health, and standing, and his friends insist his suffering must be punishment. From the middle of that darkness he makes his boldest statement of faith: I know that my Redeemer lives, and will stand at last upon the earth.
reflection: The marrow of Job's comfort lies in the little word 'my' — my Redeemer — and in the fact that the Redeemer lives. What is gold in the mine to me? It is gold in my purse that buys bread. A Redeemer who is not mine would be no comfort at all. You may hold Him with a feeble hand and half think it presumption to say 'He lives as my Redeemer' — yet faith like a mustard seed entitles you to say it. Doubts sting the soul like wasps; but if I know that Jesus lives for me, even the night is light about me.
today: Somewhere in today, say the two words Job leaned on — 'my Redeemer' — and let the ownership sink in.

context: Paul, imprisoned in Rome near the end of his life, writes a final letter to Timothy, his young colleague in Ephesus. Anticipating hardship ahead, he urges him: be strong, not in yourself, but in the grace that is in Christ Jesus.
reflection: Christ has grace without measure in Himself, but He has not kept it for Himself. As a reservoir empties itself into the pipes, He pours out His grace for His people. He stands like a fountain, always flowing — but flowing in order to fill empty pitchers and thirsty lips. Like a tree, He bears fruit not to hang on the boughs but to be gathered by those who need it. Whether grace is needed to pardon, cleanse, strengthen, or restore, it is had from Him freely. Draw on it daily with the boldness of someone taking money from their own purse.
today: Before the day's first demand, ask Christ for the specific grace it requires — patience, courage, honesty — as plainly as you would fill a pitcher.

context: Psalm 149 calls Israel to sing a new song in the assembly of the faithful: let Israel rejoice in Him that made him, let the children of Zion be joyful in their King.
reflection: Be glad of heart — but take care that your gladness has its spring in the Lord. Every attribute of God can become a fresh ray in the sunlight of gladness. That He is wise should make us glad, who know our own folly; that He is mighty, we who tremble at our weakness; that He is unchanging, we who change every hour. This gladness is a deep river, and we have only touched its brink. And there is more — all He has done in the past is a theme for song. As new mercies flow day by day, let thanksgiving continue.
today: Choose one attribute of God — His patience, His constancy, His wisdom — and let it be your reason for gladness today.

context: Paul writes to the believers in Colossae, urging them to set their minds on things above because they have been raised with Christ. He names Christ simply 'our life': the source and centre of everything they now are.
reflection: Christ is the source of our life — the same voice that called Lazarus from the tomb raised us to newness of life. He is the sustenance of our life: nothing else satisfies the hunger of the spirit. He is the solace of our life: in trouble, His presence is our consolation. He is the object of our life: as a ship speeds toward port, the believer moves toward Him. And He is the pattern of our life: living near Him, we grow like Him. Where the same life is within, the same character will, in time, show without.
today: At one point today ask, of whatever you are doing: is this drawing from Christ's life, or only from my own reserves?

context: Paul is writing to the Christians in Rome about life in the Spirit amid suffering. In this chapter of groaning and hope he states the believer's confidence: all things work together for good to them that love God.
reflection: On some points a believer is absolutely sure. He knows an unseen hand is on the world's tiller, and that wherever the ship drifts, God steers it. He knows God is wise, so there can be no accidents, no mistakes, nothing that ought not to arise. And this is not merely a theory but observed fact: every event so far has been worked toward good — the bitter medicine has worked the cure, the surgeon's sharp cut has helped the healing. Believing that God rules all and brings good out of evil, the heart is steadied, and can meet each trial calmly as it comes.
today: Take the one circumstance you would most like to edit out of your life right now, and hand it, unedited, to God.

context: In ancient Israel, lots were cast to settle decisions and disputes. This proverb takes that picture of pure chance and overturns it: the lot is cast into the lap, but the whole disposing of it is of the Lord.
reflection: If the disposal of the lot is the Lord's, whose is the arrangement of our whole life? If a simple cast of the lot is guided by Him, how much more the events of our years — for the very hairs of your head are numbered, and not a sparrow falls without your Father. Remembering this brings a holy calm. When we are anxious we cannot pray with faith; we take up the providing work and neglect the obeying. Be wise: attend to the obeying, and let God manage the providing. He will not let His child go without while His storehouse is full.
today: Do the duty in front of you and leave one outcome — just one — entirely in God's hands today.

context: Jonah, having fled from God's call and been thrown into the sea, prays from inside the great fish. His prayer ends with the sentence that sums up his rescue: salvation is of the Lord.
reflection: What Jonah learned in the deep, we can learn on dry land: rescue and every grace are the Lord's work from first to last. If I am prayerful, God makes me prayerful; whatever good I hold, it is His gift; if my life holds together, it is because He upholds it. My help comes from heaven's hills — without Jesus I can do nothing, as a branch cannot bear fruit unless it abides in the vine. This is not discouraging but freeing: I find in God all that I need, and I am not asked to be my own rock.
today: Each time something goes right today, credit it upward before you credit it inward.

context: Psalm 66 is a public song of praise recounting what God has done for His people and for the psalmist personally. It closes with gratitude for one particular mercy: God has not turned away my prayer, nor His mercy from me.
reflection: Look back honestly at your prayers and you will wonder that God ever answered them. Remember how cold they have been — faint and few, far from the wrestling that says, 'I will not let You go unless You bless me.' Remember how infrequent, except in trouble: often at the mercy-seat when needs pressed, forgetful when deliverance came. Yet, wonderful to say, God has heard these cold prayers, and not only heard but answered them. When we neglected the mercy-seat, God did not desert it. Let His kindness in hearing such prayers as ours warm us into praying more.
today: Recall one prayer God has already answered, and thank Him for it today before you ask Him for anything new.

context: God has just promised David, through the prophet Nathan, that his house and kingdom will be established. David responds not by adding conditions but by praying the promise back: do as thou hast said.
reflection: God's promises were never meant to be treated as waste paper; they were intended to be used. Nothing pleases the Lord better than to see His promises put in circulation — to see His children bring them to Him and say, 'Lord, do as You have said.' Do you think God will be the poorer for giving you what He promised? Faith takes hold of a promise and does not linger, wondering whether it is true; it goes straight to the throne and pleads it. Never let a promise rust. The sun is not weary of shining, nor the fountain of flowing; it is God's nature to keep His word.
today: Find one plain promise in scripture and pray it back word for word: 'Lord, do as You have said.'

context: The writer of Hebrews has been showing that the Son is greater than the angels. The angels' own role is then defined: they are ministering spirits, sent out to serve those who will inherit salvation.
reflection: Angels are the unseen attendants of God's people; they bear us up in their hands lest we dash our foot against a stone. Loyal to their Lord, they take a deep interest in the children of His love — there is joy among them over one person who turns home. Though unseen by us, heaven is still open, and they ascend and descend upon the Son of man. If our eyes could be opened, we would see horses and chariots of fire about the servants of the Lord. How well kept we are — and it is Christ who makes it so, for His camp surrounds those who fear Him.
today: Walk through today assuming you are more guarded than you feel; God's unseen care is not less real for being unseen.

context: Psalm 111 praises God's works in the assembly of the upright: His provision, His justice, His redemption of His people. The psalmist adds that God has commanded His covenant for ever — His commitments do not expire.
reflection: God's people delight in His covenant — not a contract He might revisit, but a commitment He has commanded for ever. Time does not erode it; our stumbling does not dissolve it; He is not a man, that He should lie, nor change His mind about His word. In it they find all things provided: God their portion, Christ their companion, the Spirit their comforter. It stands because grace is its basis and grace its topstone — a treasury of wealth, a granary of food, a fountain of life, a charter of peace, and a haven of joy.
today: When something feels uncertain today, name one thing God has committed to that cannot be revoked, and stand on that instead.

context: In the upper room on the night before His death, Jesus gives His disciples the picture of the vine and the branches: abide in me, for the branch cannot bear fruit of itself.
reflection: When have you been most fruitless? Has it not been when you lived farthest from the Lord Jesus — when prayer slackened, when your own progress engrossed your attention instead of your Lord? The sun is a great worker in fruit-making among the trees of the orchard, and Jesus is more so among the trees of His garden. Any practice that draws you near to Him will help you bear fruit, because it is from Him that all your fruit proceeds. The more simply we depend upon His grace, the more we bring forth. Trust Jesus for fruit as well as for life.
today: Return to the habit that once drew you nearest to Christ — the early walk, the open Bible at breakfast — and keep it today.

context: Early in Mark's gospel, a leper kneels before Jesus and says, 'If thou wilt, thou canst make me clean.' Moved with compassion, Jesus reaches out, touches the untouchable man, and says, 'I will; be thou clean.'
reflection: Leprosy yielded to no human remedy, but it fled at the Lord's 'I will.' Jesus speaks and it is done. Notice that He touched the leper. The man had pressed in past every regulation, and Jesus, far from rebuking him, crossed the line Himself to meet him — taking the defilement of that touch so the man could take cleanness. That is His way with all who come: He is not reluctant, and His willingness is the point of the story. The hand that multiplied the loaves and caught sinking Peter still reaches toward those who kneel and ask.
today: Bring Jesus the thing you assume He would rather not touch, and let His 'I will' answer your 'if you will.'

context: Psalm 51 is David's prayer after the prophet Nathan confronted him over his sin with Bathsheba. Beyond forgiveness, David asks for repair: create in me a clean heart, O God, and renew a right spirit within me.
reflection: Renewal calls for the same graces as beginnings: we needed repentance then, we need it now; we needed faith to come to Christ at first, and only the like grace brings us back. Let personal weakness be an argument for prayer, not an excuse for sleep. David, when he felt powerless, did not fold his arms or close his lips; he hastened to the mercy-seat with 'renew a right spirit within me.' Whoever prays this sincerely will prove it by using the means God works through: be much in prayer, live much in His word, and watch the places where you slipped before.
today: Take one worn-down area of your walk with God and pray six specific words over it: renew a right spirit within me.

context: Paul opens his letter to the Ephesians with a long sentence of praise: blessed be the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, who has blessed us with all spiritual blessings in heavenly places in Christ.
reflection: All the goodness of the past, the present, and the future, Christ bestows on His people. His obedience, His suffering, His rising, His pleading at the throne — whatever flows from them is ours by His own gift. He bears His people's names on His heart and remembers their cause. His majesty in heaven He employs for the benefit of those who trust Him; His high estate is as much at our service as His lowliness was. He who gave Himself for us in the depths of woe does not withdraw the gift now that He is enthroned on high.
today: Live today as someone already provided for; before asking God for more, count three blessings already given in Christ.

context: In the Song of Solomon, the bridegroom delights in his bride: 'Thou art all fair, my love; there is no spot in thee.' Christians have long read this love song as a picture of Christ's delight in His people.
reflection: The Lord's admiration of His people is very wonderful. He does not merely call them fair, but 'all fair.' He views you as washed clean and clothed in His own righteousness, and He considers you full of beauty. This is not flattery from a distance; it is the settled opinion of the One who knows you completely. The failings you see, He has removed; the loveliness He sees, He Himself conferred. And He is not ashamed of this opinion — He sets a 'behold' before it and invites attention to it. The voice that knows you best calls you fair.
today: When self-reproach starts today, let Christ's verdict outrank yours; He has seen everything and still says 'all fair.'

context: Psalm 126 is a song of ascents remembering the return from captivity, when Israel was like them that dream and mouths were filled with laughter. The people confess together: the Lord has done great things for us, whereof we are glad.
reflection: Some are sadly prone to dwell more on what they have gone through than on what God has done for them. Ask about their life of faith and you will hear the conflicts and afflictions, with scarcely a word of the mercy that carried them. It is true that we endure trials; it is just as true that we are delivered out of them. We have been in the Slough of Despond — but we came through it, and not on our own. The deeper the troubles, the louder the thanks; griefs are the bass part of life's song, not the melody.
today: Tell someone one specific thing God has done for you — plainly, briefly, the way the psalm does.

context: Peter and the apostles, arrested for preaching, stand before the Jerusalem council. Their defence is a declaration: God has exalted the Jesus you crucified to His own right hand, as Prince and Saviour, to give repentance and forgiveness.
reflection: Jesus, once crucified, dead, and buried, now sits in the highest place heaven affords — and His exaltation is not a private honour. So close is His union with His people that what He has, He shares: the honours He wears are held for those who are His. Be content, then, to live unknown for a little while, and to walk a weary way through thin times or uphill ones. The measure of your life is not its present obscurity. Look up to Him now by faith; the One at the right hand of God has not forgotten whose names He carries.
today: Do today's most thankless task well, unbothered by who notices; your standing is settled at a higher court.

context: In Isaiah, Zion complains: 'The Lord hath forsaken me, and my God hath forgotten me.' God answers with a love beyond a mother's and a startling image: behold, I have graven thee upon the palms of my hands.
reflection: He keeps His promise a thousand times, and the next trial makes us doubt Him. He is never a dry well, never a setting sun — yet we are vexed with anxieties as if our God were a mirage in the desert. Hear His answer: how can I have forgotten you, when I have graven you on the palms of my hands? And notice — it does not say 'your name.' 'I have graven thee': your person, your circumstances, your weaknesses, your wants, everything about you, set where He cannot look without seeing you. Will you say again that God has forsaken you?
today: Look at your open hand once today and remember the hands that carry your whole story, engraved where He always sees it.

context: Psalm 92 is a song for the sabbath day, praising God morning and evening: 'Thou, Lord, hast made me glad through thy work: I will triumph in the works of thy hands.'
reflection: Do you believe your sins are forgiven? Then what a glad person you ought to be — able to live above the common troubles of the world. And while you are glad, be grateful and loving. Show love in practical tokens: if there is a Mephibosheth anywhere, lame and overlooked, help him for Jonathan's sake; if there is a tried believer, weep with him and help carry his cross. Cheerful holiness is the most persuasive of sermons, and all the world is a pulpit for it — but the Lord must give it, so seek it in the morning before you go out.
today: Let forgiveness set your tone today, and pass it along: one concrete kindness to someone who cannot repay you.

context: Paul is encouraging the Corinthians to give generously for the poor believers in Jerusalem. Instead of commanding, he points to Christ: though He was rich, yet for your sakes He became poor, that you through His poverty might be rich.
