reflection: The Lord Jesus was eternally rich, glorious, and exalted — yet for your sakes He became poor. Had He remained on His throne and we in our ruin, there could have been no fellowship between us. So the rich kinsman bestowed His estate on His poor relatives; He gave what was His and took what was ours. In giving and receiving, the One descended from the heights so the other could ascend from the depths, and the two could embrace. Every good thing we hold in God we hold because He chose poverty to hand it to us. Herein is love.
today: Give something today that actually costs you — time, comfort, convenience — remembering the wealth that was spent on you.

context: God speaks through Isaiah to Israel, pictured as a wife who feared she was cast off: 'Thy Maker is thine husband; the Lord of hosts is his name; and thy Redeemer the Holy One of Israel.'
reflection: Jesus the Redeemer is altogether ours, and ours for ever. Every role He holds, He holds on our behalf: the shepherd's staff, the captain's sword, the prophet's mantle — all are ours. He has no dignity He will not employ for our good, no prerogative He will not exercise for our defence. He leaves us His manger, to show how God came down to man, and His cross, to show how man may go up to God. He is not ashamed to call Himself ours, though He is King of kings. Whatever today holds, you do not meet it unclaimed.
today: When you feel on your own today, remember you are spoken for; bring the day's needs to the One who claimed you.

context: Psalm 104 surveys creation with wonder: springs, birds, grass for the cattle. Among its lines stand the great trees: 'The trees of the Lord are full of sap; the cedars of Lebanon, which he hath planted.'
reflection: The cedars of Lebanon owe their planting entirely to the Lord, and their watering too — they stand on the high rock, untended by human irrigation, yet the heavenly Father supplies them. No mortal power shelters them from storm; they are God's trees, kept and preserved by Him alone. So it is with the believer. You are not a hot-house plant, sheltered from every wind; you stand exposed — and the broad wings of the eternal God cover what He Himself has planted. Like the cedars, be full of sap: green even amid winter's snows, because your roots are in His keeping.
today: Stand in today's weather without pretending it is mild; trust the One who planted you to keep what He planted.

context: After decades of waiting, Sarah bears Isaac in her old age, exactly as God had promised. She names her wonder aloud: 'God hath made me to laugh, so that all that hear will laugh with me.'
reflection: It was beyond nature that aged Sarah should hold a son; and it is beyond all ordinary rules that grace should take root and bear fruit in soil as dry as ours. Well may the mouth be filled with joyous laughter at what God has done. And Sarah's laughter spread — all who heard laughed with her. Let joy do the same: surprise your family with your peace, delight your friends with your gladness, let the cheerfulness of daily conversation speak for itself. Bunyan tells of Mercy laughing in her sleep — no wonder, when she dreamed of Jesus. Joy in God is meant to be shared.
today: Let one person today catch your gladness rather than your stress; joy given by God is meant to be overheard.

context: Speaking through the prophet Isaiah, God addresses Israel in exile, weary and doubting his care. He promises to pour water on thirsty ground and his blessing on their children, the way rain revives a dry field.
reflection: When a believer falls into a low, sad state, he often tries to lift himself out of it with dark and doleful fears. That is not the way to rise from the dust but to stay in it; you might as well chain an eagle's wing to make it soar. Fear does not bring a wanderer home — the sweet drawing of love does. Are you thirsting for the living God? Then here is exactly the promise you need: I will pour water upon him who is thirsty. Water refreshes; you shall be refreshed. Water makes fruit ripen; you shall be made fruitful. Your desires will be met to the full reach of your need.
today: When you feel dry today, do not scold yourself into life. Ask plainly for what you lack, and let the asking be enough.

context: Psalm 112 describes the person who fears the Lord and delights in his commands. Among the marks of that steady life is this: bad news does not shatter them, because their heart is fixed, trusting in the Lord.
reflection: You need not dread the arrival of bad news. Others have no God to run to; they have never proved his faithfulness as you have, so it is no wonder alarm bows them down. But you have been given a living hope. If you give way to fear the moment trouble is announced, you will be unable to meet it with the calm composure that steadies you for duty and sustains you in adversity. Your wisest course is the one Moses took at the Red Sea: stand still and see the salvation of God. Trust in the Lord, wait patiently for him, and let not your heart be troubled.
today: When the phone brings unwelcome news today, take one breath before reacting, and remember whose faithfulness you have already proved.

context: The Song of Solomon is a poem of love between bride and bridegroom. Here the bride hears her beloved approaching, leaping over the mountains toward her, and the sound of his coming fills her with joy. Christians have long read it as a picture of God's love for his people.
reflection: 'My beloved' — a golden name the people of God have always given to the one their soul loves. Even in long winters, when everything in the garden had withered, they still found room to sing of their well-beloved. Though the saints of old had never seen his face, he was their consolation and hope; and we still speak of Christ as the best beloved of our soul, precious, the chief among ten thousand. So real is this love that the apostle dares to defy the whole universe to separate us from it: neither hardship, distress, peril, nor sword has ever been able to do it. In all these things we are more than conquerors through him who loved us.
today: Somewhere in today, say the word 'beloved' back to God — not as theology, but as address.

context: Jude closes his short letter with a doxology: praise to God, who is able to keep you from falling and to present you faultless before his presence with great joy. After a letter full of warnings, all safety finally rests on God.
reflection: In some sense the path of faith is very safe; in another, no road is so dangerous. If we were sure-footed mountaineers this would not matter so much, but in ourselves how weak we are — a straw may throw us, and a pebble can wound us. We are children taking first steps in the walk of faith, and our heavenly Father holds us by the arms. If we are kept from falling, how we should bless the patient power that watches over us day by day. So with a deep sense of our own weakness we may hold a firm confidence in our safety: glory to him who is able to keep us from falling.
today: Where you feel most likely to stumble today, do not rely on your own footing; ask, in a sentence, to be held.

context: Psalm 97 is a hymn of God's kingship. The psalmist announces that the Lord reigns over all the earth and calls the whole world, out to its farthest islands, to rejoice in that reign.
reflection: There is no cause for disquiet so long as this sentence stands true: the Lord reigns. His power controls the rage of the wicked as readily as the rage of the sea; his love refreshes the poor with mercy as easily as it refreshes the earth with showers. His glory can be seen even in the fall of empires and the crash of thrones. In all our conflicts and troubles we may behold the hand of the divine King, and that is why the earth can rejoice. Whatever shakes, the throne does not. Soul, forget not, amid your pains: God over all forever reigns.
today: Read one alarming headline today against one fixed fact — the Lord reigns — and let the second frame the first, not the reverse.

context: Isaiah speaks to Jerusalem under threat of invasion. He promises that the glorious Lord himself will be to his people what a broad river was to an ancient city: abundance, provision, and a defense no enemy ship can cross.
reflection: Broad rivers bring fertility; the land beside them is known for plentiful harvests. God is all this to his people. Having him, they have abundance — what can they ask that he will not give, what need can they name that he will not supply? If you are limited, you are not limited in him. And broad rivers were of old a defense; what a defense God is to his own. The enemy cannot cross this river, and no hostile ship shall pass upon it, for God abides unchangeably the same. Trouble may prowl along the banks, but it cannot cut off the supply.
today: Name your most pressing lack out loud this morning, then set it beside this: you are not limited in him.

context: At the temple in Jerusalem, Jesus describes himself as the good shepherd who knows his sheep. Of them he says: I give unto them eternal life, and they shall never perish, neither shall any man pluck them out of my hand.
reflection: Do not think or speak lightly of unbelief, for mistrusting God's love and faithfulness grieves him. It is contrary to every promise of his word that you should be forgotten or abandoned. Can a woman forget her nursing child? They may forget, he says, yet I will not forget you. The mountains shall depart and the hills be removed, but my kindness shall not depart from you. The shepherd's word stands: no one is able to pluck his own out of his hand, and the Father, who is greater than all, holds them too. Banish the fears that dishonour such a promise, and let the life within you express itself in confident rest.
today: Each time doubt of God's care surfaces today, answer it with one line: he has not forgotten me.

context: In Psalm 62 David is surrounded by men who would gladly bring him down, yet he answers them with stillness: my soul, wait thou only upon God, for my expectation is from him.
reflection: If you look for anything from the world, it is a poor expectation indeed. But if you look to God for the supply of your needs, your expectation will not be a vain one. Constantly you may draw from the bank of faith and have your need supplied out of the riches of his lovingkindness. Better to have God for a banker than all the wealth of the world. He never fails to honour his promises, and when we bring them to his throne he never sends them back unanswered. Therefore wait only at his door, for he ever opens it with a generous hand. At this hour, try him anew.
today: Before checking anything else this morning, bring one need to God first, and let everything else come second.

context: By the lake of Gennesaret, Jesus borrows Simon Peter's boat to teach the crowd. Then he tells the fisherman, who has toiled all night and caught nothing, to launch out into the deep and let down the nets.
reflection: These fishermen were no raw hands; they understood their work. Had they lacked industry? No, they had toiled. Perseverance? They had toiled all night. Was the sea empty? Certainly not, for as soon as the Master came, the fish swam to the net in shoals. What, then, was the reason for the empty boat? Only this: apart from the presence of Jesus, means of themselves are unavailing. Without him we can do nothing; with him the same nets, the same water, the same hands are filled. His presence confers the success. So go out to today's work looking up in faith; he who bids you let down the net can fill it.
today: Before starting the task that has resisted you, pause and ask Christ into it — then let down the net again.

context: The bride in the Song compares her beloved to an apple tree among the trees of the wood: she sits under his shadow with great delight, and his fruit is sweet to her taste. The picture is of nearness enjoyed, not merely admired.
reflection: Scripture speaks of faith under the emblem of all the senses. It is sight: look unto me and be saved. It is hearing: hear, and your soul shall live. It is touch, by which the woman reached out for the hem of Christ's garment. And it is taste: how sweet are your words to my taste, sweeter than honey to my lips. Hearing the truth, understanding it, admiring it — each is faith at work. But the deepest enjoyment comes when Christ is not only heard about but received, made the very food of the soul. Then we sit under his shadow with great delight and find his fruit sweet to our taste.
today: Do not just skim a verse today; take one line slowly, the way you would taste rather than swallow.

context: Psalm 138 is David's psalm of thanksgiving. He foresees even the kings of the earth praising God after hearing his words, singing in the ways of the Lord, for great is the glory of the Lord.
reflection: Believers begin to sing in the ways of the Lord when they first lose their burden at the cross. Bunyan's pilgrim gave three great leaps there and went on his way singing. Do you remember when your own fetters fell off — what a sweet season it was when the weight of guilt lifted? One pastor recalled being so joyful at his first forgiveness that he could scarcely keep from dancing, and wanted to tell every falling snowflake the story of his deliverance. But song is not only for the beginning. As long as believers live, they keep finding fresh cause to sing, for his kindness is constant: new mercies demand new songs.
today: On the way to work, recall one specific mercy from your own story, and let it set the tone of the day.

context: Peter writes to believers scattered across Asia Minor, urging them to long for pure spiritual nourishment the way newborns long for milk — if indeed they have tasted that the Lord is gracious. The appetite grows from the taste.
reflection: 'If you have tasted that the Lord is gracious.' That little word 'if' is worth examining, but no one should be content to live under it. A humble self-distrust may raise the question in a believer's heart, but to stay in doubt would be a real loss. Do not let 'perhaps' and 'maybe' feed your soul. Move beyond the dreary ifs; do not settle in the wilderness of doubts and fears. What you have tasted of his goodness, taste again until the question quiets, and say with conviction: I know whom I have believed, and I am persuaded that he is able to keep that which I have committed unto him.
today: Trade one 'maybe God is good' for one remembered proof that he has already been good to you.

context: Through the prophet Malachi, God speaks to an Israel grown weary and cynical after the return from exile. His answer to their drift is his own constancy: I am the Lord, I change not; therefore ye sons of Jacob are not consumed.
reflection: It is well for us that amid all the changes of life there is one whom change cannot touch. All things else are changing, but with God there is no variableness, neither shadow of turning. Whatever his qualities were of old, they are now: his power, his wisdom, his truth are unchanged, and he is unchanged in his love. The delight a sailor feels when he steps from a tossing sea onto solid shore is the believer's satisfaction in resting on this truth. The wheel of circumstance keeps turning, but its axle is unchanging love. He has ever been his people's refuge in the day of trouble, and he is their sure helper still.
today: List what has changed in your life this year, then write four words beside the list: he has not changed.

context: Psalm 91 celebrates the safety of the one who dwells in the secret place of the Most High. The psalmist speaks to whoever has made the Lord their refuge and their habitation — a settled home, not an occasional shelter.
reflection: Israel in the wilderness was continually on the move. Whenever the pillar of cloud halted, the tents were pitched; but soon the trumpet sounded and they were journeying again, never long in one place. Yet they had an abiding home in their God. So the believer knows no change with regard to God. You may be well today and unwell tomorrow, at ease today and distressed tomorrow, but there is no change in your relationship to him. If he loved you yesterday, he loves you today. Prospects may be blighted and hopes disappointed, but you have lost nothing of what you have in God. A pilgrim in the world, you are at home in him.
today: Wherever the day moves you — desk, car, waiting room — practice saying: this is a location; God is my home.

context: Paul writes to Gentile believers in Ephesus who had once been outsiders to Israel's promises. Through Christ, he tells them, they are strangers and foreigners no longer, but fellow citizens with the saints and members of the household of God.
reflection: What does it mean to belong to God's household? It means we live under his good government: our daily prayer is 'your will be done on earth as it is in heaven,' and we obey the King gladly, not grudgingly. It means we share the family's standing — already children of God, with Christ for our brother and God for our Father. And it means we share the family's joys: is there rejoicing over one who returns home? We rejoice too. If we carry such a citizenship, let our walk and actions be consistent with the dignity of it — not to earn the belonging, but because it is already ours.
today: Act once today like a member of the household rather than a guest: take responsibility for something you usually leave to others.

context: In Corinth, Paul has met resistance and has reason to be afraid. The Lord speaks to him in a night vision: be not afraid, but speak, for I am with thee, and I have much people in this city.
reflection: Corinth did not look promising, yet the Lord said, 'I have much people in this city.' God was at work there before Paul ever arrived, in streets and lives that seemed unlikely soil. That is the great encouragement for anyone who tries to do good: you do not go alone, and you do not go first. The word you carry, the kindness you offer, the patience you spend on someone hard to love — none of it is wasted, for God is faithfully drawing people to himself in places we would have written off. Take courage from what steadied Paul: not the mood of the city, but the presence of the Lord in it.
today: Look once today at a person or place you have quietly written off, and assume God is already at work there.

context: Psalm 17 is a prayer of David under pursuit. He asks God to show his marvellous lovingkindness — to save, as he always does, those who put their trust in him from those who rise up against them.
reflection: When we give our hearts with our gifts, we give well; and this is how the Lord always gives. His favours come with the love of his heart. He does not send cold leftovers from a distant table; he sits beside us, not despising our poverty nor blaming our weakness. The manner of his giving is as precious as the gift itself. He gives liberally and adds no reproach — not one hint that we are a burden to him, not one cold look. There is sincerity stamped on all his kindness, and he draws us close even while he provides. This is lovingkindness that deserves the word marvellous.
today: Give one thing today the way he gives: warmly, without reluctance, and without making the receiver feel small.

context: In Leviticus, God instructs Israel's priests about the altar of burnt offering: the fire on it must be kept burning and never allowed to go out. Every morning the priest adds wood and tends the flame.
reflection: Keep the altar of private prayer burning; everything else in the life of faith borrows its fire here. Let your times alone with God be, if possible, regular, frequent, and undisturbed. Have you nothing to pray for? Consider your own soul, your family, your neighbours, your work, and the wider world. If the fire burns dimly and the chariot wheels drag heavily, do not despair — tend it, as a priest tended the flame each morning. It will not burn if the Lord does not keep it burning, so ask for his help; and use Scripture as fuel for your heart's fire, for its words are live coals. Above all, be much alone with Jesus.
today: Set an actual time and place for prayer tomorrow morning, and treat it as tending a fire, not checking a box.

context: Paul opens his second letter to Corinth by blessing the God of all comfort. He has been through affliction in Asia beyond his strength, and he testifies: as the sufferings of Christ abound in us, so our consolation also abounds by Christ.
reflection: There is a blessed proportion here. Providence holds a pair of scales: in one side, his people's trials; in the other, their consolations. When the scale of trial is nearly empty, the scale of comfort sits at about the same; when trials weigh heavy, the comfort weighs just as heavy. When the clouds gather most, the light shows the brighter. One reason is that trouble makes more room: the spade of trouble digs the reservoir of comfort deeper. Another is that in trouble we deal most closely with God — no prayer is half so hearty as the one that rises from the depths. Nearness to God is happiness, and trials bring us near.
today: If you are in a hard stretch, watch today for the comfort that comes matched to it, and name it when it arrives.

context: Psalm 33 praises God as maker and ruler of all things. From heaven the Lord looks down and beholds all the sons of men — nothing on earth, and no one, is outside his attention.
reflection: No figure of speech shows God in a more gracious light than this: he stoops from his throne to attend to the wants and behold the sorrows of ordinary people. He numbers the very hairs of our heads, marks our path, and orders our ways. When a tear is wept by you, do not think God fails to see it; as a father pities his children, so the Lord pities those who fear him. Your sigh can move his heart; your whisper can incline his ear; your prayer can stay his hand. However small your life may feel today, the Lord thinks upon you. Great as he is, he knows how to stoop.
today: Assume you are seen today. Let one private effort — done well with nobody watching — be enough because God beholds it.

context: On his deathbed Jacob blesses each of his sons. Of Joseph — attacked and grieved by archers, yet unbroken — he says: his bow abode in strength, and the arms of his hands were made strong by the mighty God of Jacob.
reflection: The strength God gave Joseph was real strength — not boasted valour that ends in smoke. Why did Joseph stand against temptation, and stand under betrayal? Because God gave him aid; there is nothing we can do without it. And notice how familiarly the strength was given: the arms of his hands were made strong by the hands of God — as if God laid his own hands upon Joseph's hands and stretched his arm along Joseph's arm, the way a father steadies a child. The archers may have grieved you too, and shot at you, and wounded you; yet your bow can abide in strength. Only remember, as Joseph did, where the strength comes from.
today: Before the hardest thing on today's list, ask for steadying — his hands on your hands — and then begin.

context: On a mountain in Galilee, the risen Jesus gives his disciples their commission to go to all nations. His final recorded words in Matthew's gospel are a promise: lo, I am with you alway, even unto the end of the world.
reflection: It is well there is one who is ever the same and ever with us — one stable rock amid the billows of the sea of life. Do not set your heart on rusting, moth-eaten, decaying treasures; do not build your house on the shifting quicksand of circumstance. Found your hope on the rock that stands immovable under descending rain and roaring floods. Every flower in the world's garden fades in its turn; but tell your secrets to the friend who sticks closer than a brother, and trust your concerns with him who will never leave you. 'Lo, I am with you alway' is enough for a soul to live upon, whoever else departs.
today: Carry the sentence 'I am with you always' into the most ordinary hour of today — the commute, the dishes, the inbox.

context: Psalm 65 is a harvest song. David praises the God who visits the earth, waters it, and crowns the year with his goodness, so that the very paths where he passes drop fatness — abundance trailing behind him.
reflection: Many are the paths of the Lord that drop abundance, but a special one is the path of prayer. Souls starve at a distance from the mercy seat and become like parched fields in a drought. Be much alone with God and you will have much assurance; spend little time alone with Jesus and your faith will stay shallow. This soul-enriching path is open to the very weakest; no high attainments are required, and you are not invited because you are advanced, but freely, as you are. So be often in the way of private devotion — Elijah on his knees drew rain onto famished fields — and sit under his shadow until you are satisfied.
today: Take the long way once today — a walk, a quiet detour — and use those minutes for unhurried prayer.

context: John writes to assure believers of what the apostles saw with their own eyes and now testify: the Father sent the Son to be the Saviour of the world. Love did not begin with us, he insists, but with God.
reflection: It is a sweet thought that Jesus did not come without his Father's consent and blessing. He was sent. We too easily credit the kindness of our salvation to the Son alone, as if the Father stood back reluctant; but at Bethlehem, in Gethsemane, and at Calvary, the Father and the Son are equally engaged in the work of love. If Jesus came, did not his Father send him? Consider the depth of love in the Father's heart when he gave his Son for the great errand of mercy. Let every thought of Jesus be connected with the God who sent him — in the Saviour's coming, see the Father's own heart.
today: When you thank Jesus today, thank the Father in the same breath — the sending was his love too.

context: Jerusalem is under siege and Jeremiah is shut up in prison, yet God gives him a promise for the future of his people: I will rejoice over them to do them good, and will plant them in this land assuredly with my whole heart.
reflection: How heart-cheering is the delight God takes in his people. We cannot see any reason for it in ourselves; we are conscious of our failures and often groan under them. Yet as the bridegroom rejoices over the bride, so the Lord rejoices over us. We never read that God delights in the cloud-capped mountains or the sparkling stars, but we do read that his delights are with the sons of men. Who could have conceived of the Eternal One bursting into song? Yet it is written: he will rejoice over you with joy, he will rest in his love, he will joy over you with singing. Let gratitude answer such a declaration.
today: Hold one thought against every self-criticism today: God's posture toward you is delight, and he intends your good.

context: After Israel repents at Mizpeh and God routs the Philistines, Samuel sets up a memorial stone between Mizpeh and Shen. He names it Ebenezer, the stone of help, saying: hitherto hath the Lord helped us.
reflection: The word 'hitherto' is like a hand pointing back down the road. Twenty years or seventy — through poverty and wealth, sickness and health, at home and abroad, in joy and in trial — hitherto the Lord has helped. Look down the long avenue of your years as down an aisle of green trees: branches of mercy overhead, strong pillars of lovingkindness bearing up your joys, and surely birds singing in those boughs of help received. But the word points forward too; whoever writes 'hitherto' is not yet at the end of the road. More needs are coming, and more help. He who has helped you thus far will help you all your journey through.
today: Set up one Ebenezer today: write down a moment God carried you, and put it somewhere you will see it again.

context: An angel appears to Joseph in a dream concerning Mary's child: thou shalt call his name Jesus, for he shall save his people from their sins. The name itself — the Lord saves — carries the mission.
reflection: When a person is dear, everything connected with him becomes dear for his sake, and so it is with the names of Christ. Every title of the Master is sweet in the believer's ear — Emmanuel, Wonderful, Counsellor — but if one name is sweeter than another, it is Jesus. It is woven into the very fabric of our songs; it is the life of many of our joys. A whole song in a word; an ocean for meaning, though a drop for brevity. Names grow precious as the person grows precious, and this name means what it says: he saves. Let it be music to your ear because it has been mercy to your life.
today: Say the name Jesus slowly once today, remembering it is not a syllable of habit but a summary of rescue.

context: In Proverbs 1, wisdom calls aloud in the streets and most refuse to listen. The chapter ends with her promise to the one who does listen: whoso hearkeneth unto me shall dwell safely, and shall be quiet from fear of evil.
reflection: Divine love shows most clearly when it shines in the middle of hard times, like a lone star smiling through rifts in the thunder clouds, or an oasis blooming in a wilderness of sand. When famine covered the land in Elijah's day, God still kept a brook for his prophet; and when the brook dried, he had a further place of provision ready. Draw the quiet inference: come what may, those who listen to God are kept. Not spared every storm, but held within it — quiet from fear of evil, which is a deeper safety than the absence of trouble. Stay yourself on his promise and rest in his faithfulness.
today: When worry starts its spiral today, interrupt it with the brook: God has kept his people before, and he knows how to keep you.

context: Paul has just described the fruit of the Spirit to the churches of Galatia — love, joy, peace, and the rest. He draws the practical conclusion: if we live in the Spirit, let us also walk in the Spirit.
reflection: Two things belong together in the life of faith: believing and walking. You will never find true faith unattended by true godliness, and you will not find a truly holy life that does not have living faith at its root. Faith is the foundation; a consistent life is the house built upon it — and of what use is a bare foundation to a man in the day of storm? He needs a roof over his head as well as rock under his feet. Let faith and life be put together, like the two sides of an arch, and they make something enduring; like light and heat streaming from the same sun, they are alike full of blessing.
today: Pick one thing you say you believe and give it legs today — one visible act that matches the trust.

context: In the Sermon on the Mount, Jesus teaches his disciples to pray with three plain verbs: ask, and it shall be given you; seek, and ye shall find; knock, and it shall be opened unto you.
reflection: There is a place in England where a portion of bread is given to every traveller who simply asks at the door. So it is at the door of God's mercy: whoever is hungry has only to knock and have his wants supplied. The fountain there is always full, and nothing that is truly good for you will be denied. If all this is to be had by knocking at mercy's door, then knock this morning, and ask large things of a generous Lord. Do not leave the throne of grace until your needs have been spread before him. No shyness need hold you back when Jesus invites; no unbelief should hinder when Jesus promises.
today: Ask God today for the thing you keep deciding is too big, or too small, to mention.
